items.title: Federal Independent Dispute Resolution Operations
items.reason: This is a Federal Register final rule document directly implementing No Surprises Act provisions, specifically the IDR process, disclosure requirements, claim coding standards, and fee structures—core regulatory changes that revenue-cycle teams must operationalize.
items.summary: The Department of Health and Human Services finalized rules implementing key provisions of the No Surprises Act's Federal Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process, established under the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021. The rule requires group health plans and health insurance issuers to disclose specific information with initial payment or denial notices, and mandates use of claim adjustment reason codes (CARCs) and remittance advice remark codes (RARCs) on paper and electronic remittance advice for non-contracted entities. The final rule also amends requirements governing the open negotiation period before IDR initiation, IDR eligibility review, administrative and IDR entity fee collection, bundled payment definitions, and batched service handling. These operational changes directly impact how revenue-cycle teams process claims, communicate with payers, manage the dispute resolution timeline, and handle fees in surprise billing scenarios.

items.title: Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR): How New Rules Affect Oncology Practices - Oncology News Central
items.reason: The item directly addresses how No Surprises Act IDR rules are being implemented and their impact on a specific provider segment, which falls squarely within the topic's focus on IDR process and rule implementation.
items.summary: This article examines how the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process established under the No Surprises Act is affecting oncology practices. The piece discusses new regulatory rules governing IDR and their concrete operational implications for oncology providers who engage in the dispute resolution mechanism. Oncology practices are directly affected as they navigate IDR requirements when disputes arise over out-of-network billing, making this relevant to understanding sector-specific impacts of federal balance-billing protections on provider revenue cycles.

The article documents industry pushback against regulatory decisions related to the No Surprises Act's implementation, likely addressing provisions affecting balance billing, IDR processes, or network adequacy standards. items.title: AHA provides recommendations to CMS on proposed rule for interoperability standards and prior authorization for drugs - American Hospital Association
items.reason: The item directly addresses CMS prior authorization reform and interoperability standards, which are core components of the topic definition covering federal prior-authorization regulatory changes.
items.summary: The American Hospital Association submitted recommendations to CMS regarding a proposed rule on interoperability standards and prior authorization for drugs. The AHA's input addresses federal regulatory changes designed to streamline prior-authorization processes through interoperability requirements. This engagement with CMS's proposed rule is significant for revenue-cycle teams because changes to prior-authorization standards and drug-approval workflows directly impact claim submission timelines, payment delays, and administrative burden. The recommendations from major healthcare organizations like the AHA help shape the final regulatory requirements that providers must implement.

items.title: OIG: 3 Largest MA Insurers Deny Prior Auth Requests at High Rates for Long-Term Acute Care, Inpatient Rehab - MedCity News
items.reason: This item directly addresses payer prior-authorization practices and denial rates for major insurers, which is a core component of prior authorization reform affecting revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) released findings showing that the three largest Medicare Advantage insurers deny prior authorization requests at notably high rates for long-term acute care and inpatient rehabilitation services. The report documents concerning denial patterns by these major payers, which directly impacts revenue-cycle teams' ability to obtain timely approvals and process patient admissions to post-acute care settings. These high denial rates highlight operational challenges in the prior authorization process and may inform discussions around payer accountability and potential regulatory reform. Revenue-cycle teams managing MA patients face increased authorization delays and denials, requiring additional follow-up efforts and potentially affecting cash flow and patient access to necessary care.

also.title: OIG: Frequent MA prior authorization denials for long-term care hospitals, inpatient rehab - Fierce Healthcare

items.title: House Panel Moves to Block CMS’s AI-Driven Prior Authorization Program: What the WISeR Rider Could Mean for Providers - JD Supra
items.reason: This item directly addresses a federal CMS prior-authorization program and legislative action to block it, which is a major payer policy shift affecting prior-authorization requirements.
items.summary: A House panel is moving to block CMS's AI-driven prior authorization program through the WISeR (Wise Enforcement of Standards in Healthcare Reviews) rider legislation. The rider would prevent CMS from implementing or expanding an artificial intelligence-based prior authorization system that the agency has been developing. This legislative effort reflects provider concerns about automated prior-authorization decision-making and represents a significant federal intervention in CMS's authority over prior-auth processes. If enacted, the WISeR rider would directly impact how payers and CMS handle prior-authorization requirements, potentially preserving existing human-review processes and delaying or eliminating AI-driven automation in authorization determinations.

items.title: West Virginia Changes Prior Authorization Law After Man's Treatment Delay and Death - AJMC
items.reason: West Virginia's state-level prior authorization law change directly fits the topic definition of state changes to payer prior-authorization requirements affecting revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: West Virginia enacted legislation reforming its prior authorization requirements in response to a case where treatment delays contributed to a patient's death. The state law change modifies how payers must process prior authorization requests, affecting the authorization workflows that revenue-cycle teams manage when seeking approval for patient treatments. This state-level reform is relevant to healthcare revenue-cycle teams because it alters the prior-authorization procedures they must follow when working with payers operating in West Virginia, potentially reducing approval delays and improving claims processing timelines.

items.title: Latest prior auth survey shows promised reform remains elusive - American Medical Association
items.reason: This AMA survey directly addresses the status of prior authorization reform implementation, which is a core component of the topic definition covering major payer policy shifts and reform progress.
items.summary: The American Medical Association released a survey finding that prior authorization reform initiatives have not yet delivered the promised improvements for healthcare providers and patients. The survey indicates that despite regulatory changes and payer commitments to streamline prior-authorization processes, barriers remain in place that continue to complicate care delivery and revenue cycle operations. This development is significant for revenue-cycle teams because persistent prior-authorization delays directly impact claim processing timelines, cash flow, and administrative costs, meaning promised efficiencies have not yet materialized in practice.

items.title: Medicare Advantage in 2026: Premiums, Out-of-Pocket Limits, Supplemental Benefits, and Prior Authorization - KFF
items.reason: The article directly addresses Medicare Advantage prior authorization changes for 2026, which is a major payer policy shift affecting CMS-regulated plans and directly impacts revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: KFF analysis covers Medicare Advantage plan changes effective in 2026, including modifications to prior authorization requirements alongside premiums, out-of-pocket limits, and supplemental benefits. The prior authorization component reflects ongoing payer policy shifts within the MA program under CMS oversight. This affects revenue-cycle teams managing authorizations for the approximately 28+ million Medicare Advantage beneficiaries, as plan-specific prior auth rules and processes will change with the 2026 plan year. Changes to prior auth procedures directly impact claim submission timelines, denial rates, and appeals workflows for healthcare providers billing MA plans.
